title: Time Blocking and Timeboxing
source: Productivity research (Cal Newport, Nir Eyal, productivity science)
content:
Time blocking is the practice of pre-scheduling specific blocks of time for specific tasks or task categories. Rather than working from a to-do list reactively, you assign every task to a specific time slot in your day.

Core principle: A calendar is not just for meetings — it is your daily operating plan. Every hour of your workday should have an intentional purpose.

How to time block effectively:
1. Start with your most important work (MIT — Most Important Task) when your energy is highest. For most people, this is within 2 hours of waking.
2. Block 'deep work' periods: 1.5-3 hour blocks with zero scheduled interruptions
3. Cluster shallow work (email, Slack, admin) into designated windows — not scattered throughout the day
4. Schedule buffer time between blocks: transitions take 5-10 minutes, and blocks rarely end exactly on time
5. End-of-day planning: spend 10 minutes planning tomorrow's blocks before shutting down

Timeboxing vs. Time blocking:
- Time blocking: assign a type of work to a time slot (e.g., 'deep work 9-11am')
- Timeboxing: assign a specific task to a fixed box with a deadline (e.g., 'write report draft, 90 minutes max')
- Timeboxing leverages Parkinson's Law: work expands to fill available time. Constraints improve focus and output quality.

The weekly plan: Before each week, identify your top 3-5 outcomes for the week, then schedule blocks that move toward those outcomes. This ensures daily actions align with weekly priorities.

Handling disruptions:
- Keep a 'task parking lot' for unexpected work that arrives during deep work blocks
- Reschedule blocks in real time rather than abandoning the system when disruptions occur
- Protect the first deep work block of the day most aggressively — it sets the tone

Research findings: People who plan their day in advance accomplish 48% more than those who don't (Cali Lewis, productivity research). The planning investment of 10 minutes saves 2 hours of inefficient work.
